data.title: Asean, India Unlikely to Impose Retaliatory Tariffs After Trump's AnnouncementMarket Talk
data.description: Retaliatory tariffs from Asean members and India seem unlikely after U.S. President Trump's punitive tariffs hit Asia, says Deepali Bhargava, ING regional head of research for Asia-Pacific in a note. However, uncertainty remains as countries continue to negotiate, she writes. Economies such as Vietnam made last-minute efforts to avoid tariffs, she notes. "Unfortunately, they were unsuccessful in escaping sweeping tariffs," she says. Vietnam is likely to continue negotiating with the U.S. by making further concessions, she adds. These include lowering import duties on a range of goods, such as cars, liquefied gas and certain agricultural products, she says.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

data.title: Eurowind Energy, Autoliv sign 12-yr PPA in Romania
data.description:
BUCHAREST (Romania), April 3 (SeeNews) - Danish renewable energy company Eurowind Energy said on Thursday that it has signed a 12-year power purchase agreement (PPA) in Romania with Sweden-based car safety equipment maker Autoliv.

The PPA, whose value was not disclosed, involves the delivery of clean electricity from Eurowind Energy's 48 MW wind park in Romania's southeastern commune of Pecineaga, Constanta county, which is currently in development, the company said.

Once completed, the Pecineaga wind farm will generate an annual output of around 176,000 MWh, enough to cover the energy needs of 48,000 Romanian households, as per the press release.

"We still expect the park to be in operation by the end of 2025, but as you can read in the PR, the agreement with Autoliv contains a buffer saying in operation by 2027. This is normal procedure, should unexpected incidents occur," the email accompanying the press release reads.

"PPAs are essential in developing new energy parks. Power Purchase Agreements with large, ambitious companies like Autoliv ensure the continuous development of new parks and a cleaner future for all of us," Eurowind Energy commercial director Jesper Schaumburg-During said.

Autoliv was established in 1997 through the merger of the Swedish company Autoliv AB and Morton Automotive Safety Products, a division of U.S.-based Morton International. It is now present in 25 countries worldwide.

Founded in 2006 and headquartered in Hobro, Denmark, the Eurowind Group employs over 650 people in fifteen European countries and the U.S. Part of Eurowind Group, Eurowind Energy entered the Romanian market in 2011. Last month, Eurowind Energy said it is developing a wind project with a planned final capacity of around 1,200 MW in Romania. The company also plans to operationalise the 60.2 MW Teius photovoltaic park in Alba county this year.

data.title: Euro High-Yield CDS Jumps to 8-Month High After U.S. Tariffs AnnouncementMarket Talk
data.description: The cost of insuring euro credit against default using credit default swaps climbs, with high-yield CDS hitting an eight-month high, as President Trump's tariff announcements raise concerns about prolonged trade disputes and weaker economic growth. "Global financial markets experienced a sharp selloff as Trump's unexpectedly aggressive efforts to reshape the global economic landscape rattled investors," Tickmill Group's Patrick Munnelly says in a note. The iTraxx Europe Crossover index which tracks euro junk bond credit default swaps rises 13 basis points to a high of 340bps, S&P Global Market Intelligence. The iTraxx Europe Main index which tracks euro investment-grade CDS rises 3bps to 67bps. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

data.title: India's Shilpa Medicare gains on launch of new version of cancer drug
data.description:
** Shares of Shilpa Medicare rise 4.7% to 678.5 rupees

** Co's partner, Amneal Pharma, launched IV administered-version of its cancer drug Boruzu

** Trading volume of ~500,000 shares is 1.4x its 30-day avg

** Stock has shed 20% so far this year

data.title: Pandora shares lose sparkle after Thailand tariff
data.description:
** Shares in Denmark's Pandora slump around 12% after U.S. President Donald Trump announced sweeping new tariffs, including on Thailand, the jewellery maker's only production hub

** Imports from China will be hit with a 34% tariff on top of the 20% Trump previously imposed, while Thailand faces 36%

** Pandora's CFO Anders Boyer in February said a 10% tariff on imports to the U.S. from Thailand would have an annual impact of 350 million to 400 million Danish crowns

** The stock is the worst performer on Europe-wide STOXX 600 index; if losses hold it will see its worst day since March 2020

data.title: India's Bank of Baroda rises after upbeat Q4 performance
data.description:
** Shares of Bank of Baroda up 2.6% at 237.60 rupees

** Bank's global advances grew 12.9% y/y to 12.31 trln rupees ($143.9 bln) as of March 31, as per provisional figures

** Global deposits rose 10.3% y/y to 14.72 trln rupees as of March 31

** Stock set to gain for third straight session

** BOB down ~1% YTD

($1 = 85.5440 Indian rupees)

data.title: INTERVIEW WITH GOV. JOSH SHAPIRO (D-PA) AS AIRED ON MSNBC`S THE 11TH HOUR
data.description:
TRANSCRIPT

April 02, 2025

NEWS EVENT

GOV. JOSH SHAPIRO (D-PA)

INTERVIEW WITH GOV. JOSH SHAPIRO (D-PA) AS AIRED ON MSNBC`S THE

11TH HOUR

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

20 East Thomas Road, Suite 2200

Phoenix, AZ 85012

asc.info@viqsolutions.com

Copyright 2025. Provided under license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

All materials herein are protected by United States copyright law

and/or license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc., and may not be

reproduced, distributed, transmitted, displayed, published or

broadcast without the prior written permission of

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

You may not alter or remove any trademark, copyright or other

notice from copies of the content.

INTERVIEW WITH GOV. JOSH SHAPIRO (D-PA) AS AIRED ON MSNBC`S THE

11TH HOUR

APRIL 2, 2025

SPEAKERS:

GOV. JOSH SHAPIRO (D-PA)

STEPHANIE RUHLE, MSNBC HOST

STEPHANIE RUHLE, MSNBC HOST: Earlier today, I sat down with Pennsylvania Governor Josh Shapiro for an exclusive keynote conversation to see how he thinks Trump's tariffs will impact Pennsylvania.

(BEGIN VIDEOTAPE)

GOV. JOSH SHAPIRO (D-PA): I think Trump is pushing a button and raising costs on people. Listen, you got a background in finance. Most Americans don't — the stock market can be confusing. The ups and downs of inflation can be confusing. This is super simple. Donald Trump pressed a button, raised costs, started a trade war with our allies, which is going to further raise costs on the American people and it's going to make people's lives worse.

RUHLE: Take us to PA, whether you're talking big business here or this little brewery that we're in.

SHAPIRO: Yeah.

RUHLE: How will this impact businesses and lives?

SHAPIRO: Look, so let's talk about this brewery, right? Pennsylvania, very proud of the fact that we are second in the nation in the number of craft breweries. Now, I'm competitive as hell. I want us to be first, but this is big business for Pennsylvania. Big important businesses for our main streets, right? So let's take the beer you drink here out of a can. That can is subject now to a 20 or 25 percent tariff.

The malted barley that goes into making the beer is subject to a tariff. And the vats that you use to brew the beer, they're made of steel, they're subject to a tariff.

There's no way these small businesses that line our main streets, that serve us these great craft beers, are going to be able to not pass those costs — cost increases along to consumers. So from the beer you drink to the cars you buy, to the Harley's that you want to ride, things are going to cost more for Pennsylvania companies and Pennsylvania consumers.

RUHLE: But not everyone agrees, especially in this area, right? Bethlehem Steel, just a few miles away from where we are, they used to be one of the biggest steel producers in the world. Huge business, great manufacturing jobs, for many, many reasons, they shut down. But one of them was foreign trade. So you've got people that are steel makers and aluminum producers and they like the ideas of tariffs.

SHAPIRO: There is no question that the bad trade deals of the '90s and the early 2000s hurt Pennsylvanians. But making things cost more, as Donald Trump wants to do right now, is not the answer. Take a look at this brewery. Let's go small business, large business. This brewery is doing everything that Donald Trump, in theory, wants people to do, right? Buy American, source your materials from America, line our main streets with American businesses. They're doing everything they need to do here.

And by the way, they're not exporting any of this beer to Canada or anywhere else. And guess what? Things are going to cost more. The largest — now, let's go to big business. The largest hydroelectric manufacturing facility in North America is in York, Pennsylvania. So they make those big turbines, right, that go in the water that generate power. We say we want to generate more power in this country. I certainly want to in Pennsylvania.

The steel that they need to build those turbines is steel that they source, not from Canada where most of our steel aluminum comes from, but domestically here in America. And guess what? That steel costs more because Donald Trump has injected through this tariff war chaos in both our domestic and our foreign markets. And guess what happens when you inject that kind of chaos and you create that fluctuation in the market, prices go up.

Put it another way, if you're driving down a windy road at night and it's foggy, what do you do? You pull over. You hold onto your capital, right? So now we're not investing capital, stuff costs more even for companies that are domestically operate it, domestically run, and domestically source their raw materials. In addition to that, we're shutting off markets for Pennsylvania companies and importantly, Pennsylvania farmers. Our number one export market for Pennsylvania farmers, Pennsylvania dairy farmers is Mexico.

Those markets are either going to be shut off or the products that we make here in Pennsylvania are going to cost so much more that our farmers are going to suffer. So I get — I'm sorry, pardon me — I get that the trade deals of the nineties screwed over Pennsylvanians in many ways, screwed over people in other states across this country. But what Donald Trump is doing right now is making it harder. And his argument that we just want to bring jobs back to America, something I support, is not going to come as a result of this approach to tariffs.

RUHLE: So, if he has got the wrong idea, what is the right idea? Because there are people in Pennsylvania who say, my town is hollowed out. There aren't opportunities. I might not love what Donald Trump is doing, but let me roll the dice because he's doing something.

SHAPIRO: So first, if you're going to use tariffs to try and help bring things back to America, it's got to be far more surgical. What Donald Trump is proposing across the board is just going to be a massive tax increase on American — the American people and American companies. What do we need to do? We need to invest again in these communities, invest in our main streets, work hard to help companies that are here grow, build data centers, produce more energy so we have more opportunities to grow here.

The same things that I am trying to do as governor of Pennsylvania, that Donald Trump is now trying to make harder. In addition to that, if you want to be able to grow economically in this country, don't hollow out our science and technology systems, don't hollow out our education systems. The kind of cuts that Donald Trump is pushing forward right now is going to make it harder for us to compete in the STEM areas, in technology and so many other areas five years from now, six years from now, seven years from now, because of the decisions they're making here.

So it's dumb to have this across the board tariff. We're raising taxes on the American people and companies, and it's even dumber to kind of hollow out our system, so we can't compete in the future. So Trump is coming at this in the exact wrong way and it's going to hurt Pennsylvanians.

RUHLE: You mentioned farmers getting hurt. The White House is toying with this idea that they may offer some support, another sort of Farm Aid Bill, which they had to do in Trump's first administration.

SHAPIRO: Back in 2018.

RUHLE: Do you support the idea of a Farm Aid Bill? Because I know you don't want us spending more money, but I know you don't want your farmers to get hurt.

SHAPIRO: OK, so the farmers are getting hurt because Donald Trump is starting a tariff war —

RUHLE: Yes.

SHAPIRO: — and shutting off markets for these farmers. Remember back in his first term, the money they generated from the tariffs, they used to pay the farmers.

RUHLE: Yes.

SHAPIRO: Because they hurt the farmers. We think about that, that cyclical nature —

RUHLE: But assuming he — the tariffs happen, do you want him to offer a farm aid package?

SHAPIRO: What we've seen from him so far are efforts to hurt our farmers. Let me explain specifically, beyond the tariffs. We've got a pretty simple initiative here in Pennsylvania, it only costs $13 million. In a multi-trillion dollar federal budget, $13 million is not even around the area (ph), it's smaller than that. That $13 million goes to paying 189 farms in Pennsylvania to produce their produce, to produce their meats and their dairy, their poultry, to support our food banks in Pennsylvania, to support those people who are hungry. Donald Trump cut that.

He hurt 189 farms and he hurt folks who were going to bed hungry. I got one out of every five kids in Pennsylvania goes to bed hungry at night, relies on these food banks that now don't have locally produced foods from local farmers because Trump cut $13 million. So when you ask me, do I want to support our farmers? Yeah, but I want to make sure they're not being hurt and Donald Trump continuously hurts our farmers. I give a damn about our farmers in Pennsylvania. Trump's making their lives way worse.

RUHLE: I want to ask you about frustration because the administration's argument around what they're doing is going to cause short-term pain for long-term gain. Another way to look at short term is transitory. Transitory is what the Biden administration, how they talked about inflation. They said it's just going to be short term. And the American people didn't want to hear it.

SHAPIRO: Right.

RUHLE: Didn't want anything — they — transitory, they were throwing it out the window. What do you think of the fact that we're now hearing using a different word, but the same concept from this administration and a whole lot of people are saying, OK, let's give them some time.

SHAPIRO: Look, I was critical of the so-called Bidenomics or whatever hell they called it back in the Biden administration, just as I'm critical of what Trump's trying to do here. Listen, here's the thing. Like I don't have an economics degree and I don't have time to like spend time listening to all these smart economists and professors and others. I'm worried about the good people of Pennsylvania who are getting absolutely screwed by this administration. Right?

Eggs cost more, blueberries cost more, ground beef costs more when you go to the market. This was supposedly an election about how are we going to bring down costs. And today, on whatever it is, Liberation Day or whatever Trump is calling it, we're now going to see prices go further up. So they're making excuses now. They're hiding behind a few economists that they can wrap their arms around and give all these theories to the American people. I don't live in theory, I live in reality. And the reality is Donald Trump's economic policies are making things harder for the good people of Pennsylvania.

RUHLE: When you say Bidenomics or whatever the hell they called it, you sound annoyed with Democrats.

SHAPIRO: I didn't like the whole Bidenomics thing years ago. I spoke out about it then. And again, I'm speaking out about what I think is dangerous about Trump's economic policies. I've been pretty even handed on that because I'm trying to create opportunities for the good people of Pennsylvania. Those opportunities are minimized when things cost more, when people can't get ahead, when people can't get by. And the federal government has been making it harder for people to get by.

(END VIDEOTAPE)

RUHLE: We have much more of my keynote conversation with Governor Shapiro. When we come back, we're talking about the Democratic win in Wisconsin and how it could be paving the path to victory for the rest of the party, or at least he hopes so.

(COMMERCIAL BREAK)

RUHLE: Yesterday's big win in Wisconsin supreme court race was a shot in the arm for Democrats. Remember, Wisconsin is one of the most 50/50 states in the country, one of only three states with a Senator from each party. And the race last night wasn't even close, a 10-point victory. Then there is Lancaster County, Pennsylvania. It went to Trump by 16 points just five months ago. But last week, a Democrat won a state Senate seat there for the first time in almost 50 years.

I asked Governor Josh Shapiro about what all of this moment means for Democrats.

(BEGIN VIDEOTAPE)

RUHLE: Let me ask you about the special elections that took place yesterday, both in Wisconsin and Florida. What does that tell you about where the country is?

SHAPIRO: Well, we had a little bit of experience on this a week prior in Lancaster County, an overwhelmingly Republican community. This is a district — state senate district that Donald Trump won by, I think it was 15 or 16 points. Now, two years earlier, I won it. But we had a special election where nobody gave the Democrat a chance, other than me and some other folks working here on the ground. It was a district that was going to the polls as part of a referendum on Donald Trump and the chaos he's brought over the last 70, 75 days to Pennsylvania and across the country.

And guess what happened? Voters went to the polls and the underfunded Democrat won in a district that Donald Trump just a few months ago carried by 15 or 16 points, sending a clear message that the path you are on, the chaos Donald Trump is injecting in our lives every day is not something that this Republican community in Lancaster County supports.

We saw it again last night in Wisconsin, a state that Donald Trump won just a few months ago. The Democrat running for the supreme court statewide race won overwhelmingly, I think in the end by about 10 points.

You saw in these two districts in Florida where Republicans were winning by 30 or more points, the Republican won by a much smaller margin. When I talk to Democrats right now and they're, worried, they're anxious, they're angry, you name it. The thing I say to them is you're not helpless. You have agency. You've got power here. Sometimes we're seeing them exercise their power when they show up at a town hall meeting, and we're seeing them exercise that power when they show up at the ballot box, and they're rejecting the chaos of Donald Trump.

RUHLE: Then let me ask you, because when you talk about that special election in Pennsylvania or the fact that you've got people here that vote for you and vote for Donald Trump, what is the right path for the Democratic Party? Because even in the last few weeks, a few of the names who've been emerging as some of the most outspoken are those who are looking to resist, a Bernie Sanders, an AOC, and their message is different from what I'm hearing from you, which is stand up and fight back, but also find a lane to work with this administration.

SHAPIRO: Yeah, look, I don't think that these are mutually exclusive. I'm glad folks are showing up to rally with Bernie and the Congresswoman. I am glad that folks are showing up at the ballot box for these special elections. I'm glad that folks are calling their Congressmen and writing letters and sending emails and posting online, because I want people to feel like they have agency. I think for me, as governor of Pennsylvania, the most important thing I can do is deliver for my Commonwealth, which is ultimately sort of the ultimate swing state, if you will, in the nation.

I'm one of the only governors in the country with a divided legislature. So for me to accomplish all the things we've accomplished, it required votes from Republicans and Democrats in the legislature. I'm going to continue to focus on common sense, on results-oriented governance. You know I live by three letters here in Pennsylvania. We focus every day on getting it (ph) done for the good people of Pennsylvania. That's my focus. That's the direction I'm heading in.

RUHLE: Then before we go, is it frustrating for you? Right. Your tagline is get it (ph) done, and in some ways it seems like to some folks that DOGE has kind of tried to steal that idea. So they're trying to tell the American people, we're cutting costs, we're cutting the fat, we're getting blank done, when that's actually what you're doing every single day here.

SHAPIRO: Yeah.

RUHLE: And people might not realize it.

SHAPIRO: Well, we do it surgically. We do it thoughtfully, and we do it with results in mind. Let me explain. Prior to serving as governor and attorney general, I ran Pennsylvania's third largest county as the chairman of the county commissioners in Montgomery County. First thing I did there was zero base budgeting. Instead of taking the budget from the year before and just tacking on a couple of percent or cutting a couple percent, we actually started every single agency with a budget of zero.

And we said, tell us what your mission is and tell us what it costs to advance that mission for the people of our county. And guess what happened? Some agencies saw their budgets go way up, some saw it go way down. Overall, spending went down and output went up. We surgically reduced costs while increasing the output for folks in that county.

As governor, we've taken on a similar approach. We've cut costs and we've made investments where it matters most. We've cut taxes for the people of Pennsylvania. So I'm someone who wants to see government be trimmed, money go back in people's pockets, but you got to do it surgically and you've got to do it with a mindset of being more competitive. So I'll invest in our classrooms, I'll invest in those small businesses because I want to be competitive today and tomorrow, while also making sure we trim our bureaucracy.

And we're making our bureaucracy work at the same time. And that's an important part of this. Let me give you an example. We cut costs in our government, but we increased investment in getting people answers more quickly. Today —

RUHLE: Then why did people vote for Donald Trump in this state? If everything you're laying out — if I'm buying everything you're selling, why did he win here?

SHAPIRO: Donald Trump waged a campaign where he promised change, he promised costs will be cut, and he's delivered on neither. He's raised costs and he's injected what I would consider to be chaos, not change, but I just want to come back to what I was saying before. What I find people want is, yeah, waste to be eliminated from government, but government to work.

The day I got sworn in as governor two years ago, this business, if they applied for a business license, this brewery would've taken them eight weeks to get that business license. Today, on my watch, it takes just two days to get that same business license. So we're cutting costs, right? We're putting money back in people's pockets, but we're investing in a government that actually works for them, that actually delivers results, that actually get it (ph) done, and that is what people want.

RUHLE: Governor, thank you for your time.

SHAPIRO: Thank you.

(END VIDEOTAPE)

RUHLE: You can catch my full interview with Governor Shapiro on our "11th Hour" YouTube page, and I'm pretty sure we don't bleep out the curses on YouTube. Just go to msnbc.com/stephanie.

END

data.title: European banks slide on recession worries after sweeping US tariffs
data.description:
** Shares in European banks fall on Thursday after U.S. President Donald Trump unveiled new tariffs, stoking worries about economic growth of the world's largest economy

** The STOXX 600 banks index (.SX7P) was down 3.3% at 0807 GMT, the worst performer among other sectoral indices on STOXX 600 index

** Barclays strategists note that while tariffs risk was largely priced in, recession risk less so

** (...) if recession fears increase and rates expectations get repriced lower, we believe that the sector may see some profit taking given high positioning on the space," they add

** Germany's Deutsche Bank and Commerzbank drop around 5% each

** Asia-exposed banks such as Standard Chartered , HSBC slip 7% and around 5%, respectively

** Italy's BPER Banca , UniCredit (CDRI.MI) are down around 4%

** France's Credit Agricole , Societe Generale , BNP Paribas slip around 3%

** Spain's Sabadell , CaixaBank Santander , Bankinter , are down between 2% and 3%

data.title: Australian beef farmers say Trump tariffs to boost price of US hamburgers
data.description:
Key points:
  • Australian beef industry to pass on price increases to US consumer
  • Australia receives identical 10% tariff to export rivals Brazil and Argentina
  • Government to help access new markers
  • Donald Trump renowned as consumer of McDonald's

At a cattle auction in Australia's lush Southern Highlands, an auctioneer shouts from a gantry over the din of nearly 2,000 cows waiting to be bought.

Prospective owners in felted hats bid up prices with a nod of the head, and business is brisk, despite U.S. President Donald Trump on Thursday imposing a 10% import tariff on Australian goods and singling out the country's beef exports for criticism.

Australian beef farmers, traders and industry groups said on Thursday they would pass on the extra costs of U.S. tariffs to the American consumer, pushing up the prices of hamburgers and steaks.

"The effect on our market here today ... we haven't seen it," said Dhugald McDowall, of livestock agency Elders Cleary McDowall, at the Southern Regional Livestock Exchange in Moss Vale, around two hours by road from Sydney.

"It does make the commodity that's going into America a lot dearer for their own consumers. So I think in the short term, it could be quite detrimental to the U.S. economy."

Australia exports beef worth a record A$4 billion ($2.52 billion) annually to the United States - its largest market - while banning U.S. fresh beef products since 2003 due to the detection of bovine spongiform encephalopathy, known as mad cow disease, in U.S. cattle.

That has drawn the ire of Trump, who said on Thursday Australia "won't take any of our beef" as he announced tariffs on the country.

PRIZED BEEF

Australian beef is prized by U.S. fast food chains for its lower fat content, which is combined with fattier U.S. beef to produce hamburgers with the ideal fat content.

Under regulations from United States Department of Agriculture, hamburgers and ground beef cannot have a fat content of more than 30%.

Garry Edwards, chair of Cattle Australia, an industry trade body, said Australian producers had seen some short-term disruption to U.S. exports in recent weeks while waiting for details on tariffs, but buyers there would ultimately be forced to pay up for Australian beef.

"They are completely reliant on our grass-fed beef and some of our high quality grain-finished beef to meet their requirements of their domestic consumers," he said.

"I can't imagine that the American consumer is going to like paying more for their burgers or their steaks for the foreseeable future."

The industry's presence in the American fast-food supply chain has been used as a negotiating tactic by the Australian government.

Australia trade minister Don Farrell said last month higher tariffs on beef could push up the price of McDonald's MCD.N hamburgers. Trump's support of the chain featured prominently in his electoral campaign.

Livestock agent McDowall noted Australia had been given an identical tariff to export rival Brazil, the world's largest beef exporter, as well as Argentina, which together with Australia and the United States comprise the world's four largest beef exporters.

"So we're all at 10%, it really doesn't change anything," he said.

Australia Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said on Thursday the government would provide support to industries impacted by the U.S. tariffs to sell into alternative markets.

"America's not the only market," said James Fitzpatrick, a cattle farmer at the auction.

"There's a worldwide market out there."

($1 = 1.5860 Australian dollars)

data.title: SEN. MARKWAYNE MULLIN (R-OK) IS INTERVIEWED ON FOX NEWS` HANNITY
data.description:
TRANSCRIPT

April 02, 2025

NEWS EVENT

SEN. MARKWAYNE MULLIN (R-OK)

SEN. MARKWAYNE MULLIN (R-OK) IS INTERVIEWED ON FOX NEWS`

HANNITY

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

20 East Thomas Road, Suite 2200

Phoenix, AZ 85012

asc.info@viqsolutions.com

Copyright 2025. Provided under license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

All materials herein are protected by United States copyright law

and/or license from VIQ Media Transcription, Inc., and may not be

reproduced, distributed, transmitted, displayed, published or

broadcast without the prior written permission of

VIQ Media Transcription, Inc.

You may not alter or remove any trademark, copyright or other

notice from copies of the content.

SEN. MARKWAYNE MULLIN (R-OK) IS INTERVIEWED ON FOX NEWS`

HANNITY

APRIL 2, 2025

SPEAKERS:

SEN. MARKWAYNE MULLIN (R-OK)

SEAN HANNITY, FOX NEWS HOST

SEAN HANNITY, FOX NEWS HOST: Anyway, here with reaction, Oklahoma Senator Markwayne Mullin is with us.

It is amazing. They don't have a leader. The only leaders that really seem to be getting any traction ,those that are getting the most attention, Bernie Sanders, AOC, Jasmine Crockett.

And I've got to be honest, Senator. I'm okay with that.

SEN. MARKWAYNE MULLIN (R-OK): Well, and there — what's funny you named three socialists. I mean, truly three socialists, and so that tells you the disarray of the Democrat Party right now.

It's amazing though because we all assumed Nancy Pelosi was the leader of the Democrat Party, and she was. Since she has left, they have completely fell apart. I mean, Cory Booker, personally, I actually get along with him, politically, we couldn't be more different.

He spoke for 25 hours, I can't think of a single thing he said other than what you said. He ranted against Elon Musk and Donald Trump. Well, that's the same playbook that the Democrat Party's had.

Hakeem Jeffries isn't leading it. Chuck Schumer isn't leading it. And their poll numbers show it, 21 percent favorability across the United States with Democrat voters. That's about as low as it gets.

show it percent favorability across the United States with Democrat voters that's about as low as it gets.

HANNITY: How did they become — explain this to me — the party that champions men's rights to play women's sports, how did they become the party that puts the rights of illegal immigrants, even Tren de Aragua, illegal immigrants over the safety of Americans? How did they become the party that thinks we have a constitutional crisis because we identify hundreds of billions in waste, fraud, abuse and corruption?

How did they become the party that can't stand for a president, a first lady, for mothers that lost their children by the hands of illegal immigrants, by the — for the wife of a slain officer, for a young man who — who beat cancer, who's 12 years old, how did they become the party of bingo paddles? How did that happen?

MULLIN: I can't explain it. It's like this, you can't argue with crazy, and nor can you explain crazy.

But this is what happens when you have career politicians that's running your party. Chuck Schumer is running their party right now. If you want to say he's a leader, the guy is out of touch with the American people. They've lost the working class, as so many of the — of my labor friends now will tell you, they didn't leave the Democrat Party, the Democrat Party left them. That is the way that the Democrat Party has been moving since Obama was in office.

And now, who is left standing is just what you said, Bernie Sanders is the leader, essentially leading the Senate right now, and he's a socialist. He literally honeymooned in Russia. And he wrote about it. That's the guy that that's leading the Senate for the Senate party.

HANNITY : He bragged about it.

MULLIN: Yes, yeah.

HANNITY: He bragged — well, that's who he's become.

Senator Markwayne Mullin, appreciate you being with us as always.

END

data.title: Oil companies slip as Trump's tariffs spark trade war and fuel demand fears
data.description:
** Shares of oil companies decline in pre-market trading after U.S. President Donald Trump announced sweeping new tariffs that investors worry will inflame a global trade war and limit fuel demand

** Brent futures were down 3% at $72.71/bbl while U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude futures decline 3.15% to $69.45/bbl by 0824 GMT; adding to decline was sharp rise in U.S. crude oil stockpiles

** Trump on Wednesday unveiled a 10% minimum tariff on most goods imported to the United States

** Import of oil, gas and refined products were exempted from the new tariffs - White House

** U.S. oil majors Exxon Mobil down 2.1% while Chevron falls 2.7%

** Oil and gas producer Occidental Petroleum down 2.5%, while top oilfield services company SLB declines 3.4%

** Europe's oil cos (.SXEP) down 1.61%

** "This (tariffs) threatens to drive up inflation and stall worldwide economic growth, with a consequent drop in demand for oil," says Ashley Kelty, analyst at Panmure Liberum

** Would expect even more volatility in the near term, as the effective tariffs fluctuate, as countries try to negotiate lower rates or enact retaliatory tariffs - Kelty

data.title: Rama Steel Tubes Posts 14% Rise in Total Sales Volume in Fiscal Q4
data.description:
Rama Steel Tubes recorded a 14% year-on-year rise in its sales volume to 54,256.30 tons in the fiscal fourth quarter ended March 31, versus 47,506.68 tons reported a year ago.

The sales volume also rose by 5% to 51,669.01 tons in the quarter ended December 2024, according to a Thursday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

data.title: Big Pharma Left Uncertain About U.S. Tariff RiskMarket Talk
data.description: Uncertainty remains across the pharmaceutical industry until there is better visibility around the potential impact of U.S. tariffs, Shore Capital analyst Sean Conroy says in a note. U.S. President Trump announced across-the-board 10% tariffs on Wednesday on all imports and a 20% tariff on EU goods which, for now, exempts pharmaceuticals. GSK confirmed that there would be no changes to its outlook, whereas AstraZeneca said it will assess the implications of Trump's tariff announcement and Roche said it would consider additional U.S. investments. Shore Capital anticipates that stock prices might remain weak until companies can quantify the potential impact of tariffs on earnings and revenue, Conroy says. GSK's shares are up 1.5%, while AstraZeneca's are up 1.6% and Roche's are down 2.1%. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

data.title: Moonpig Could Return to Growth as Cash Generation Underpins BuybacksMarket Talk
data.description: Moonpig continues to be a highly cash generative business benefiting from high profit margins, low capex requirements and negative working capital, Shore Capital analyst David Hughes says in a note. The greetings-card and gifting platform's elevated debt levels following an acquisition are now under control and this cash generation translates into an increased share buyback of 60 million pounds from an inaugural 25 million pounds in fiscal 2025, Hughes says. "Key for the business going forward will be delivering on sales in the Greetz and Buyagift divisions alongside core Moonpig brand, and with this we see the potential for a return to high-single digit growth," he says. Shares are up 1.6% at 224 pence.(anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0835 GMT - Moonpig continues to be a highly cash generative business benefiting from high profit margins, low capex requirements and negative working capital, Shore Capital analyst David Hughes says in a note. The greetings-card and gifting platform's elevated debt levels following an acquisition are now under control and this cash generation translates into an increased share buyback of 60 million pounds from an inaugural 25 million pounds in fiscal 2025, Hughes says. "Key for the business going forward will be delivering on sales in the Greetz and Buyagift divisions alongside core Moonpig brand, and with this we see the potential for a return to high-single digit growth," he says. Shares are up 1.6% at 224 pence.(anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0832 GMT - European insurers Zurich, Mapfre and Aviva could see impacts from the implications of tariffs on the U.S. auto insurance industry, UBS says in a research note, penciling in an around 4% increase in claims costs for U.S. personal auto insurers as a result of the tariffs. This translates into a 2 to 3 percentage point increase in personal auto loss ratios before any offsetting impact for price increases, analysts write. "We would expect impacts to begin to be felt in late 2025 and early 2026," they write, adding that companies are expected to pass along some costs through price hikes. Higher prices would be positive for Zurich given its U.S.-based Farmers structure though, while Aviva would be impacted through its Canada business. Admiral isn't exposed to this risk given it is selling its U.S. business. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0829 GMT - President Trump's new tariff on Thailand is worse than expected, at 37%, which will hit Pandora as the Danish jeweler has a large manufacturing footprint in the country, Citi analyst Thomas Chauvet writes. Pandora's entire jewelry output is manufactured in Thailand, and the 37% tariff is well above the 10% base case anticipated by Pandora management in February. Management guided for an EBIT headwind of between 350 million and 400 million Danish kroner based on a 10% tariff, without any mitigation measures such as pricing and/or cost efficiencies. Pandora shares are down 12%, the biggest loser on the Stoxx Europe 600 index. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0828 GMT - Malaysia's banks could see limited direct impact from the planned 24% reciprocal tariff by the U.S., as loans to exporters are insignificant, CIMB Securities' Rachel Huang says in a note. However, indirect knock-on effects on supply-chain vendors could emerge if trade volumes decline, she notes. Loan growth is projected at 5.5% in 2025 and 5.2% in 2026, but could slow if investments stall, she says. Credit costs may increase by 5 bps on top of the expected 22 bps for the sector in 2025, potentially driving cuts to average target prices by 3%-6%, she adds. Huang may revise the sector's earnings forecasts due to rising economic uncertainty following the tariff announcement. CIMB pegs Public Bank, RHB Bank and Hong Leong Bank as its sector top picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0825 GMT - Hong Kong shares ended lower, with technology and home appliances stocks leading the losses. Additional tariffs on Chinese goods carried on risk-off sentiment in the market, with investors watching for Beijing's retaliatory policies going forward. Consumer products, home appliance and tech stocks led the losses, with Shenzhou International Group falling 14% and Techtronic Industries declining 12%. Haier Smart Home lost 8.0%, Lenovo Group fell 7.8% and JD.com was 5.2% lower. Meanwhile, Chinese property stocks led the gains, with Longfor Group up 1.6% and China Overseas Land & Investment up 1.2%. The benchmark Hang Seng Index ended 1.5% lower at 22849.81 and the Hang Seng Tech Index fell 2.1%. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0819 GMT - Shares in major European oil companies fall in early trading as crude benchmarks drop as much as 3% following U.S. President Trump's latest tariff blitz. BP loses 2.1%, while Shell and TotalEnergies slip 1.6% and 1.1%, respectively. Eni is down 1%. Sweeping U.S. levies on major trade partners raised concerns over a global economic slowdown that could crimp demand for crude, prompting traders to shed riskier assets in favor of safe-haven investments. The tariffs triggered a slump in oil futures, with Brent crude down 3% at $72.73 a barrel and the U.S. oil gauge WTI falling 3.1% to $69.45 a barrel. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0817 GMT - Primary Health Properties' bid for Assura is lower than KKR's offer, but the company might hope enough shareholders are open to a lower price if this means the share capital stays in a listed vehicle, Quilter Cheviot says. KKR's bid entails having to find a new home for the money and, with PHP and Assura shares still offering a dividend yield of over 7%, some might argue the long-term prospects are greater than the cash in hand, analyst Oli Creasey says in a note. "While some investors may be tempted to take the cash and reinvest in PHP, the company simply isn't large enough for everyone to follow suit," he says. PHP shares are up 0.45% at 221.50 pence with Assura up 0.6% at 46.76 pence. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0811 GMT - Shares in European transport and logistics companies sink after President Trump announced wide-ranging tariffs on U.S. imports. The tariffs add to ongoing concerns over the outlook for global trade volumes and also raise the possibility of retaliatory actions from countries. This could increase prices and complexity further. Companies in the sector are underperforming the wider European market in early European trade. The Stoxx Europe 600 index falls 1.3%, while A.P. Moller-Maersk shares fall 7.1% in Denmark, Deutsche Post shares are 4.2% lower in Germany, and Kuehne + Nagel shares fall 3.6% in Switzerland. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0802 GMT - Wise PLC's results were roughly in line with expectations, Citi says in a research note after the money-transfer company confirmed its midterm targets and shared preliminary fiscal 2025 figures. However, the guidance that fiscal 2026 pretax profit margins should come in at the top of its 13% to 16% range, instead of above 16% as consensus expected, could disappoint, analyst Pavan Daswani writes. Despite the lack of positives, shares rose as much as 6.2%, before paring some gains. The positive reaction is likely a relief rally, Daswani adds in an emailed comment. "The shares have been down since the announcement that they were going to do a CMD only a few weeks ago and so soon before they are due to report their trading update anyway," he writes. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0800 GMT - European luxury companies fall at market open after U.S. President Trump's tariffs announcement. Luxury and sporting-goods companies are at great risk of their profit margins shrinking as a result of the tariffs, which are more severe than expected, RBC Capital Markets analyst Piral Dadhania says in a research note. The bank estimates elevated tariff impact for Burberry and Swatch, while LVMH and Moncler are the least exposed. The biggest fallers at market open include Prada and Richemont, dropping 4%, and Burberry and Swatch down 3.8%. Shares in Ray-Ban maker EssilorLuxottica lose 5.2%. (maitane.sardon@wsj.com)

0758 GMT - U.K. home builders reported improved sales rates, and with the largest players looking to open more sites in the coming months. This is a vote of confidence in the underlying market, RBC Capital Markets analysts Anthony Codling and Oliver Dyson say in a note. Still, U.K. house prices may be soft in April and May as the housing market takes a break following April's introduction of stamp duty charges, the analysts say. According to data from building society Nationwide, month-on-month house prices were flat in March and up 3.9% on year, with the average house price in the month at 271,316 pounds. Shares in Persimmon, Taylor Wimpey and Berkeley Group were up at 1.9%, 1.3% and 1.2% respectively. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

0751 GMT - Big Pharma stocks are up after U.S. President Trump exempted pharmaceuticals for now from his reciprocal tariffs on global imports. "It is still somewhat unclear whether the broader reaching 10% baseline tariffs could still be levied against imported drugs and vaccines, in our view," Shore Capital analyst Sean Conroy says in a note. Likewise, Bryan Garnier Research analysts say in a note that the pharma industry is left uncertain about an actual tariff risk given Trump's mixed remarks that drugmakers "will come roaring back to the U.S., because if they don't, they got a big tax to pay." Novartis and Sanofi are up 0.6% and 0.3%, respectively. The U.K's AstraZeneca and GSK rose 1% and 1.6%, respectively. Swiss pharma giant Roche is down 2.3% after its multiple-sclerosis drug Ocrevus failed to meet its primary endpoint. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

data.title: Trump's Tariffs Announcement 'Worse Than the Worst Case Scenario,' Wedbush Says
data.description:
The slate of tariffs from the Trump administration is "worse than the worst case scenario" feared by analysts, according to Wedbush Securities.

In a note emailed to clients on Wednesday, Wedbush analysts, led by Daniel Ives, said tech stocks will be under "major pressure" following the announcement.

On Wednesday, the Trump administration unveiled another wave of tariffs to be imposed on many countries, including China and Taiwan, as well as the European Union.

The analysts said investors will focus on the details of the tariffs over the next 24 hours but for Apple , which produces iPhones in China, the focus will be on possible exemptions from the tariff policy if it builds more factories in the US. For Nvidia and other chipmakers, the focus will be on pricing and margin impacts, the analysts said.

"[W]e continue to believe there are off ramps and major negotiations that will happen over the coming months with various countries and companies to navigate this new world of tariffs," the analysts said.

Wedbush analysts described the 34% reciprocal tariff levied on China as "the jaw dropper," followed by Taiwan's tariff rate at 32% and the EU at 20%.

China said it opposes the tariffs, calling them a "typical unilateral bullying practice," and vowed to take countermeasures to protect its rights and interests, media outlets reported, citing a statement from the country's Ministry of Commerce.

The latest 34% reciprocal tariff levied on China is in addition to the existing 20% tariff imposed on all Chinese products imported to the US.

Taiwan and the EU also said they would take countermeasures to the tariffs if negotiations with the Trump administration fail.

In a live-streamed broadcast, European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen said the EU is ready to respond and is preparing countermeasures against the US tariffs, according to reports.

data.description:
